title: Memorandum of Contract for Real Estate Wholesaling: What It Is and Why You Need One
excerpt: A memorandum of contract is one of the most powerful tools a real estate wholesaler can use to protect their deals. Learn exactly how it works, when to file it, and how it keeps sellers from walking away.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: memorandum of contract real estate wholesaling
metaDescription: Learn how a memorandum of contract for real estate wholesaling protects your deals, clouds title, and prevents sellers from backing out before closing.
metaKeywords: memorandum of contract real estate wholesaling, memorandum of contract wholesaling, cloud on title wholesaling, wholesale real estate contract, equitable interest real estate, lis pendens alternative, wholesale deal protection

sections.content: You locked up a great deal. The seller signed the purchase agreement. You spent the next two weeks finding a cash buyer and running your numbers. Then the seller calls and says they changed their mind. Without the right paperwork filed at the county courthouse, there is very little you can do. This is exactly why every wholesaler needs to understand the memorandum of contract for real estate wholesaling — and how to use it properly.
What Is a Memorandum of Contract?

A memorandum of contract (sometimes called a memorandum of agreement or notice of equitable interest) is a short, recorded document that notifies the public that a buyer has a contractual interest in a specific property. It does not reveal the full terms of your purchase agreement — especially not your purchase price — but it does create what is known as a 'cloud on title.' That cloud makes it very difficult for a seller to sell the property to anyone else while your contract is still active.

In real estate wholesaling, the gap between signing a contract and closing can be 30 to 60 days or more. A lot can happen in that window. A seller might receive a higher cash offer, get cold feet, or simply decide to stay. Filing a memorandum of contract gives you legal standing and practical leverage to keep the deal moving forward. If you are just getting started in this business, the guide on [Wholesaling Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesaling-real-estate-for-beginners) covers the full deal process from first contact to closing.
How a Memorandum of Contract Works

When you record a memorandum of contract at the county recorder's or clerk's office, it becomes part of the public record attached to the property's legal description. Any title company or attorney doing a title search will find it immediately. This means a seller cannot get a clean title commitment — which is required for any standard real estate closing — without first resolving your contract. In plain terms, the property cannot be legally sold to a third party without dealing with you first.
What the Document Typically Includes

items: The names of both the buyer (you) and the seller
The property's legal description and address
The effective date of the original purchase agreement
The expiration or closing date of the contract
A statement that the buyer holds equitable interest in the property
Notarized signatures from at least the buyer, and ideally both parties

sections.content: Pro Tip: Keep your memorandum of contract brief — typically one page. You do not want to disclose your contract price, your assignment fee, or your buyer's identity. The goal is simply to put the world on notice that a contract exists, not to reveal your deal structure.
Why Wholesalers Need This Document

Many new wholesalers skip this step because it feels unnecessary when a seller seems motivated and cooperative. That is a mistake. Motivated sellers can become unmotivated overnight — especially if they get a better offer or a family member talks them out of the deal. Markets like [Atlanta](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga), [Dallas](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx), and [Phoenix](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) are competitive, and sellers in those cities often receive multiple offers within days. Filing a memorandum of contract costs very little — usually between $10 and $50 in recording fees — but it could save you thousands of dollars in lost assignment fees.

'In wholesaling, your contract is your asset. A memorandum of contract is the lock on the door. Without it, you are just hoping the seller stays loyal — and hope is not a business strategy.' — Common wisdom among experienced wholesalers
Real-World Example

Imagine you put a distressed property under contract for $115,000. Your ARV analysis shows the home is worth $185,000 after repairs, and you plan to assign the contract for a $10,000 fee. You spend two weeks marketing the deal and find a strong cash buyer. On day 18, the seller gets a retail offer for $150,000 from a neighbor. Without a memorandum of contract on file, the seller can simply back out of your agreement and accept the higher offer. With a memorandum filed on day one, the neighbor's title company will flag the cloud immediately. The seller cannot close without your release — giving you the leverage to either negotiate or enforce your contract.
Step-by-Step: How to File a Memorandum of Contract

items: Step 1 — Sign your purchase agreement with the seller and make sure it is dated and includes a clear property description.
Step 2 — Draft your memorandum of contract. Use a template reviewed by a local real estate attorney familiar with your state's requirements.
Step 3 — Have the document notarized. Some states require only the buyer's signature; others require both parties.
Step 4 — File the memorandum at the county recorder's or clerk's office where the property is located. Bring a government-issued ID and the recording fee (typically $10–$50).
Step 5 — Keep a stamped copy for your records. This is your proof that the cloud on title exists.
Step 6 — Once your deal closes or falls apart, file a release of the memorandum immediately. Leaving it on title after your contract ends is unprofessional and can create legal issues.

sections.content: State-Specific Rules You Must Know

Not every state treats a memorandum of contract the same way. In some states, recording a memorandum without a valid underlying contract could expose you to legal liability. A few states have specific language requirements or require both parties to sign and notarize. Always consult a real estate attorney in your state before using this tool for the first time. The cost of an hour of legal advice — often $150 to $300 — is well worth it compared to the risk of filing a defective document or losing a deal entirely. If you are brand new to real estate investing overall, the guide on [Real Estate Investing for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-investing-for-beginners) provides important foundational context.
Memorandum of Contract vs. Lis Pendens

You may have heard the term 'lis pendens' used in a similar context. A lis pendens is a notice filed when there is active litigation involving a property. Some investors have tried to use it as a deal-protection tool, but filing a lis pendens without an active lawsuit is generally considered bad faith and can result in penalties. A properly filed memorandum of contract is the correct and ethical tool for wholesalers. It gives you the same cloud-on-title protection without the legal risk of an improper lis pendens.

Pro Tip: Before you run any numbers on a wholesale deal, use the free [Deal Calculator at AIWholesail](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator) to quickly calculate your maximum allowable offer, estimated ARV, repair costs, and potential assignment fee — all in one place.
Common Mistakes Wholesalers Make With This Document

items: Forgetting to file at all — hoping the deal closes without any drama
Including the purchase price, which reveals your margin to competitors and the seller
Filing too late — wait until after signatures but file the same day if possible
Never releasing the memorandum after a deal dies, creating title problems for the seller
Using a generic template that does not meet your state's specific recording requirements

sections.content: Avoiding these mistakes is simple once you build a reliable system. Many successful wholesalers treat filing the memorandum as part of their same-day contract checklist — right alongside sending the executed agreement to their title company or closing attorney. The faster you file, the sooner your equitable interest is protected.
